name: Unkulunkulu
description: Unkulunkulu, meaning "the Great Great One," is the supreme creator god in Zulu mythology. He is considered the first man, who emerged from the reeds (Uthlanga) or sometimes from a seed. He is not actively worshipped but is highly respected as the originator of all things. Unkulunkulu taught the Zulu people essential skills such as cattle-raising, agriculture, and hunting, and gave them the laws and customs by which they live. He is the source of life and death, and while not directly involved in everyday affairs, his influence is fundamental to the Zulu worldview. He is often associated with the ancestors, as he represents the ultimate ancestor from whom all Zulu people are descended. Unkulunkulu's existence is more of a concept representing the origin of everything rather than a deity who intervenes in human affairs. He is credited with creating the sun, moon, and stars, as well as animals and plants. His stories are passed down through oral tradition, connecting the Zulu people to their roots and providing a framework for understanding their place in the world. While sometimes portrayed as a distant figure, his actions laid the foundation for Zulu society and culture. He is the ultimate source of all that is, and his legacy continues to shape the Zulu way of life.
subtitle: The Great Great One; First Man

keyMyths.title: Emergence from the Reeds
keyMyths.description: Unkulunkulu emerged from the reeds, Uthlanga, as the first man. He then created all living things and taught the Zulu people essential skills.

keyMyths.title: The Chameleon's Message
keyMyths.description: Unkulunkulu sent a chameleon to tell humans they would live forever. However, he then sent a lizard with the message that they would die, and the lizard arrived first, bringing mortality to humankind.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Cattle
keyMyths.description: Unkulunkulu gifted cattle to the Zulu people, teaching them how to care for and use them, establishing their importance in Zulu culture.

sources.author: Callaway, Henry
